title: nopriv AJAX handler sync_all_clicked protected only by is_legit_ajax_call (requires admin + nonce)
description: Five AJAX actions (opt_out_of_sync, banner_close, sync_all_products, post_update banners) are registered with wp_ajax_nopriv_ hooks, meaning they are reachable by unauthenticated HTTP requests. However, every handler immediately calls WC_Facebookcommerce_Utils::is_legit_ajax_call(), which calls current_user_can('manage_woocommerce') AND check_ajax_referer(). Both checks must pass. An unauthenticated visitor has no WooCommerce capability and cannot obtain the admin nonce, so the actual exploitability is nil. The nopriv registration is an oversight (the handlers should only be admin-facing) but not an exploitable flaw.
exploitation_scenario: Not exploitable. POST to admin-ajax.php?action=wc_facebook_sync_all_products without a valid admin nonce returns 'Permission denied'.
remediation: Remove the five wp_ajax_nopriv_ registrations in PluginRender::add_hooks(). These handlers have no legitimate use case for unauthenticated visitors.
false_positive_reason: Not a false positive as a code-quality issue (unnecessary nopriv registration), but not exploitable as-is because is_legit_ajax_call enforces manage_woocommerce capability.

title: nopriv AJAX handler wc_facebook_update_signals_state — protected by nonce but cookie-manipulation risk
description: The handle_update_state AJAX handler is intentionally public (nopriv) so frontend JS can update the signal-hold cookie for anonymous shop visitors. It requires check_ajax_referer('wc_facebook_signals_state_nonce', 'security'). The nonce is embedded in the page JS for all visitors (wp_localize_script). Any visitor can call this endpoint with the nonce they already received in the page HTML to set the server-side cookie to 'held' or 'active'. The effect is toggling whether Meta pixel CAPI events are sent. There is no state written to the database; only a first-party cookie is affected (via FacebookSignalsState::hold/release which sets $_COOKIE). Impact is limited to a visitor suppressing their own pixel tracking, which is the intended use case (consent management).
exploitation_scenario: A visitor extracts the nonce from the page source and POSTs to admin-ajax.php with action=wc_facebook_update_signals_state&security=<nonce>&state=held. This holds pixel signals for their own session. No cross-user impact.
remediation: Intended behavior — no fix needed. However, document that this endpoint is public-by-design. Consider adding rate-limiting or IP-based abuse detection if signal suppression at scale is a concern.
false_positive_reason: Intentionally public. The nonce is per-page and per-session. Impact is self-limited to the caller's own session cookie.

title: nopriv AJAX handler facebook_release_signals — cookie-gated, can be bypassed to abuse CAPI relay
description: The handle() method for the facebook_release_signals AJAX action is publicly accessible with no nonce check. The only gate is a cookie check: if the wc_facebook_signals_state cookie is absent the request is rejected with 403. The cookie value is trivially obtained by first visiting any page of the site (the hold/release JS sets it automatically) or by simply including the cookie in the request. An attacker who sets any non-empty wc_facebook_signals_state cookie value can then POST arbitrary events via this endpoint. The plugin will forward up to 20 events per request (MAX_EVENTS=20) to Facebook's CAPI on behalf of the store's pixel. Events must have names in a fixed allowlist (PageView, ViewContent, AddToCart, Purchase, etc.) and must have an event_time within the last 30 minutes. User_data is restricted to a known field allowlist and is sanitized with sanitize_text_field. This allows an attacker to inject synthetic PageView, Purchase, AddToCart, etc. events into the store's Meta Pixel CAPI data, inflating analytics and conversion metrics without making real purchases.
exploitation_scenario:
1) Send a GET to any page of the shop to receive a Set-Cookie header for wc_facebook_signals_state. Alternatively, include Cookie: wc_facebook_signals_state=active in the request.
2) POST to /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php with action=facebook_release_signals and a JSON body containing events: [{event_name:'Purchase', event_time:<now>, custom_data:{value:1000, currency:'USD'}}].
3) The plugin forwards the Purchase event to the store's CAPI pixel. Repeating at scale pollutes Facebook ad reporting, wastes ad budget, and can cause Facebook to misattribute conversions.
remediation: 1) Add a proper nonce check (wp_check_referer equivalent) issued only to authenticated browser sessions that have a valid browsing context. 2) Alternatively, require the caller to supply a HMAC signed with a per-session server-side secret rather than just a cookie presence check. 3) Add rate limiting per IP. The cookie-presence check (line 73-79) is insufficient since cookies can be freely set by the attacker in the request.

title: Framework AsyncRequest nopriv handler — nonce-protected, effectively private
description: AsyncRequest registers a nopriv AJAX handler for background processing, but maybe_handle() immediately calls check_ajax_referer($this->identifier, 'nonce'). The nonce is only created server-side when dispatch() is called by admin code. An unauthenticated attacker cannot produce a valid nonce for this action.
remediation: No fix required. The nopriv registration is a common pattern for WP background processing to allow cron-context callbacks without a logged-in user.
false_positive_reason: Nonce is only created during admin-initiated dispatch; unauthenticated access is blocked at nonce verification.

title: BackgroundJobHandler nopriv _test endpoint leaks loopback probe string
description: The nopriv handler for the connection test action simply echoes '[TEST_LOOPBACK]' and exits. No authentication or nonce check. Any external party can call this to confirm the plugin is installed and the background job system is active. Not a practical security risk — reveals only that the plugin is installed, which is typically already discernible from page headers/scripts.
exploitation_scenario: GET/POST to admin-ajax.php?action=background_handle_virtual_products_variations_test returns '[TEST_LOOPBACK]'. Useful for fingerprinting plugin presence.
remediation: Add a nonce or IP check before responding. For a loopback test, restrict to requests originating from 127.0.0.1/::1. Low priority.

title: OAuth handle_connect() — unsanitized $_GET['code'] and $_GET['state'] in token exchange (phpcs suppression comment)
description: handle_connect() is protected by both current_user_can('manage_woocommerce') and a nonce check on $_GET['nonce']. The phpcs:disable comments suppress sanitization warnings for $_GET['code'] and $_GET['state'] which are passed through to a WooCommerce Connect Server via wp_safe_remote_get (URL query parameters). Values are passed through http_build_query which URL-encodes them, so SQL injection/XSS in these params is not possible. The values are never stored directly without sanitization (subsequent token_data is sanitized with wc_clean). This is a minor code hygiene issue, not an exploitable vulnerability given the existing capability+nonce gate.
exploitation_scenario: Only a logged-in WooCommerce admin completing a legitimate OAuth flow can reach this code. Not externally exploitable.
remediation: Add sanitize_text_field(wp_unslash(...)) to $_GET['code'] and $_GET['state'] to satisfy coding standards and eliminate the phpcs suppression comments.

title: handle_fbe_redirect() — open redirect via base64-decoded redirect_uri (admin-only, host validated)
description: handle_fbe_redirect() decodes a base64-encoded redirect_uri from $_REQUEST and redirects to it. Access is protected by current_user_can('manage_woocommerce'). The decoded URI is validated: the scheme must be https and the host must match a strict regex covering facebook.com, instagram.com, whatsapp.com, and commercepartnerhub.com subdomains. If validation fails, the user is redirected to site_url() instead. No nonce verification is present (phpcs:ignore comment). The open redirect risk to arbitrary URLs is mitigated by the allowlist regex. However, the regex allows subdomain patterns like *.od.facebook.com (with 5-digit prefix) and numeric subdomains which could theoretically be registerable on other platforms — a very theoretical risk given Meta controls these domains.
exploitation_scenario: An admin who is socially engineered to click a crafted link like /wp-admin/admin.php?page=...&redirect_uri=<base64 of https://attacker.facebook.com.evil.com/> would NOT be redirected because the regex rejects non-Meta hostnames. The most realistic risk is if an attacker controls a Meta-owned subdomain (near-impossible).
remediation: Add a nonce check to handle_fbe_redirect to prevent CSRF. The host allowlist is adequate, but the absence of nonce means an attacker can initiate the flow from a crafted link if the admin is tricked into clicking it during an active session.

title: WebHook REST endpoint — has permission_callback (manage_woocommerce), not open
description: The automated scan flagged this as 'REST route without permission_callback'. In fact, a permission_callback is present at line 63-65, requiring current_user_can('manage_woocommerce'). The webhook endpoint at /wp-json/wc-facebook/v1/webhook is not publicly accessible.
exploitation_scenario: Not exploitable. Requires WooCommerce manager credentials.
remediation: None required. The taint analysis tool produced a false positive.
false_positive_reason: permission_callback is explicitly defined at line 63 and requires manage_woocommerce capability.

title: Settings Handler REST endpoints — parent class permission_callback enforces manage_woocommerce
description: The automated brief flagged lines 35 and 45 as 'REST routes without permission_callback'. Both routes explicitly set 'permission_callback' => [$this, 'permission_callback']. The permission_callback method is inherited from AbstractRESTEndpoint (includes/API/Plugin/AbstractRESTEndpoint.php line 40-42) and enforces current_user_can('manage_woocommerce'). False positive.
false_positive_reason: AbstractRESTEndpoint::permission_callback() exists and enforces manage_woocommerce; both routes reference it explicitly.

title: WhatsApp Settings Handler REST endpoints — parent class permission_callback enforces manage_woocommerce
description: All three REST routes (whatsapp_settings/update, whatsapp_settings/update/integration_config, whatsapp_settings/uninstall) reference permission_callback from AbstractRESTEndpoint which enforces manage_woocommerce. False positive from automated scan.
false_positive_reason: AbstractRESTEndpoint::permission_callback() enforces manage_woocommerce for all three routes.

title: Offer Management REST endpoints — __return_true permission_callback with JWT validation
description: The three Offer Management endpoints (CreateOffersEndpoint, GetOffersEndpoint, DeleteOffersEndpoint) are registered at /wp-json/fb_api/offers with 'permission_callback' => '__return_true', making them fully public. Authentication relies entirely on a JWT in the jwt_params request parameter. The JWT is validated via RequestVerification::decode_jwt_with_retries(), which uses Facebook-issued public keys (fetched and cached via transients). The JWT must contain an 'aud' claim matching the store's product catalog ID. This design means: (1) any attacker who knows the store's catalog ID and can obtain or forge a valid Meta-issued JWT can perform coupon CRUD operations; (2) the catalog ID is sometimes discoverable from page HTML/JS; (3) JWT key material is fetched from Meta's servers and cached — if those keys are compromised or the cache is poisoned, arbitrary JWT signing becomes possible. Additionally, there are two business-logic gates: the merchant must enable Facebook managed coupons AND a Meta-controlled rollout switch must be enabled.
exploitation_scenario: 1) Discover the store's Facebook product catalog ID (often visible in page source pixel configuration). 2) Obtain a valid Meta-signed JWT with aud=<catalog_id> (requires a Meta developer account or compromised Meta credentials). 3) POST to /wp-json/fb_api/offers with the JWT to create, retrieve, or delete WooCommerce coupons without a WordPress account. Real-world exploitability depends on ability to obtain a Meta-issued JWT for the target catalog.
remediation: 1) Add an additional WordPress authentication layer (e.g., WooCommerce REST API key) as a second factor beyond the JWT. 2) Validate the JWT issuer ('iss' claim) strictly against Meta's known issuer values. 3) Consider IP allowlisting to Meta's egress ranges for these endpoints. 4) Add logging/alerting for all coupon create/delete operations via this endpoint.

title: facebook-commerce.php line 782 — $_GET['url'] echoed inside JS via esc_js+sanitize_text_field
description: The popupOrigin value is set from $_GET['url'] processed via sanitize_text_field() then esc_js(). The page containing this script block is the WooCommerce admin settings page, only accessible to administrators. esc_js() properly escapes the value for a JS string context by backslash-escaping quotes and special characters. sanitize_text_field strips HTML tags and extra whitespace. The combined output is safe: an attacker would need to be an admin to reach this page, and esc_js prevents JS injection within the string literal. The phpcs:ignore comment for NonceVerification.Recommended is present because this is a GET parameter used for display only.
exploitation_scenario: A WooCommerce admin visiting /wp-admin/admin.php?page=wc-facebook&url=javascript:alert(1) would see the value sanitized. esc_js would escape it to 'javascript:alert(1)' (backslash-escaping the colon is not guaranteed, but alert(1) within a string is harmless). The actual break scenario would require escaping the JS string context, which esc_js prevents.
remediation: Use esc_url() or validate against a URL allowlist instead of esc_js()+sanitize_text_field(). While current mitigations are likely sufficient, defense-in-depth recommends URL-specific escaping for URL values.

title: API.php log_events_for_tests — LFI via getenv('FB_E2E_LOGGER_PATH') in require_once
description: The log_events_for_tests() method constructs a file path using getenv('FB_E2E_LOGGER_PATH') and passes it to require_once. The path is: dirname(plugin_dir_path(__FILE__)) . $logger_path. If an attacker can control the FB_E2E_LOGGER_PATH environment variable (e.g., via a .env file, server misconfiguration, or PHP putenv injection through another vulnerability), they could cause arbitrary PHP files to be included. Guards exist: (1) a rollout switch must be enabled (Meta-controlled), (2) a test cookie named by getenv('FB_E2E_TEST_COOKIE_NAME') must be present and non-empty. Both guards can be satisfied if env vars are attacker-controlled. Additionally, file_exists() is checked before require_once, which limits inclusion to existing files on the server. This is primarily a risk in CI/test environments where these env vars are set, not production deployments.
exploitation_scenario: In a production environment where FB_E2E_LOGGER_PATH and FB_E2E_TEST_COOKIE_NAME are set (unusual), an attacker who can set the cookie (trivial) could trigger inclusion of the path plugin_dir/../<env_value>. If the env var is attacker-controlled (e.g., via putenv in a deserialization gadget), arbitrary file inclusion becomes possible. In typical production setups without these env vars, this code path is inert.
remediation: 1) Remove this E2E logging code entirely from production builds. 2) If kept, validate the logger path against a strict allowlist or ensure it is only non-empty in CI environments (check for WP_ENVIRONMENT_TYPE). 3) Never derive require_once paths from environment variables in production code.

title: Products.php get_enhanced_catalog_attributes_from_request — raw $_POST array_filter in product save context
description: get_enhanced_catalog_attributes_from_request() uses array_filter on $_POST to collect enhanced catalog attribute fields, then processes them with wc_clean(Helper::get_posted_value(...)). This is called from product save hooks which are admin-only (manage_woocommerce). The phpcs:disable NonceVerification.Missing comment indicates the nonce is checked elsewhere in the save flow (WooCommerce's standard product save nonce). Values are passed through wc_clean (which calls sanitize_text_field internally). No user-controlled data is used raw. False positive from automated analysis.
exploitation_scenario: Only a WooCommerce admin saving a product can trigger this. Not externally exploitable.
remediation: None required for security. The phpcs:disable comment should be accompanied by an explanation that the calling context guarantees the nonce was already verified.
false_positive_reason: Called exclusively from admin product save hooks; wc_clean sanitizes all values; nonce verified in calling context.

title: Background_Handle_Virtual_Products_Variations — SQL injection via unparameterized INSERT (DB-sourced IDs)
description: set_product_visibility_meta() builds an INSERT VALUES string by interpolating $post_id directly into the SQL (line 197: "('{$post_id}', 'fb_visibility', 'no')"). However, $post_ids originates exclusively from get_posts_to_update(), which fetches post IDs from the WordPress database via a parameterized SELECT (posts.ID). These are integer primary keys from the database, not from user input. The UPDATE query at line 238 uses array_map('intval', $post_ids) which is safe. The INSERT does not cast to int, but since the values come from the DB's own ID column, SQL injection is not possible in practice. That said, the INSERT should use $wpdb->prepare() or intval() casting as a matter of code hygiene.
exploitation_scenario: Not exploitable. Data flow: DB posts.ID -> get_posts_to_update() -> set_product_visibility_meta(). No user input in the chain.
remediation: Apply intval() to $post_id in the INSERT loop to match the UPDATE query pattern and eliminate the static analysis finding.
false_positive_reason: IDs come from the database's own primary key column, not from user input. No SQL injection possible.
